coredata.dc:title: Enhancing hydrogen storage efficiency in metal hydride tanks through conical heat exchangers and phase change material integration

coredata.dc:description: This study proposes an innovative approach to enhance the thermal management of metal hydride (MH) hydrogen storage systems by integrating a conical heat exchanger and phase change material (PCM) jacket. Magnesium-based MH reactors offer high hydrogen storage capacities, but their performance is limited by poor thermal conductivity, which hinders charging and discharging processes. The conical coil heat exchanger design improves heat transfer, reducing hydrogen charging time by 30% compared to conventional helical coils. Additionally, incorporating a PCM jacket optimizes the thermal performance, achieving up to a 69% reduction in charging time. The PCM absorbs excess heat during hydrogen charging and releases it during discharging, further improving the reactor's charging/discharging rate. Sensitivity analyses on hydrogen inlet pressure, heat transfer fluid temperature, and Reynolds number are performed, identifying optimal operating conditions. The proposed design is validated using experimental data, confirming its effectiveness in reducing charging and discharging times. To address the long-term thermal behavior of the PCM, future studies are recommended to experimentally validate PCM thermal stability over multiple hydrogen charging/discharging cycles. This integrated system offers a promising solution for enhancing hydrogen storage in clean energy applications, contributing to the development of more efficient and sustainable hydrogen fuel technologies.

head.ce:title: Enhancing hydrogen storage efficiency in metal hydride tanks through conical heat exchangers and phase change material integration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study proposes an innovative approach to enhance the thermal management of metal hydride (MH) hydrogen storage systems by integrating a conical heat exchanger and phase change material (PCM) jacket. Magnesium-based MH reactors offer high hydrogen storage capacities, but their performance is limited by poor thermal conductivity, which hinders charging and discharging processes. The conical coil heat exchanger design improves heat transfer, reducing hydrogen charging time by 30% compared to conventional helical coils. Additionally, incorporating a PCM jacket optimizes the thermal performance, achieving up to a 69% reduction in charging time. The PCM absorbs excess heat during hydrogen charging and releases it during discharging, further improving the reactor's charging/discharging rate. Sensitivity analyses on hydrogen inlet pressure, heat transfer fluid temperature, and Reynolds number are performed, identifying optimal operating conditions. The proposed design is validated using experimental data, confirming its effectiveness in reducing charging and discharging times. To address the long-term thermal behavior of the PCM, future studies are recommended to experimentally validate PCM thermal stability over multiple hydrogen charging/discharging cycles. This integrated system offers a promising solution for enhancing hydrogen storage in clean energy applications, contributing to the development of more efficient and sustainable hydrogen fuel technologies.

ce:section.ce:para: The global environmental crisis, characterized by ozone layer depletion, air pollution, bushfires, and rising greenhouse gas emissions, has intensified the search for sustainable alternative energy sources. Among these, hydrogen (H₂) has emerged as a promising candidate for clean energy. However, the widespread adoption of hydrogen-based fuel cells is hindered by challenges in hydrogen storage, which has become a critical area of research [1]. Solid-state Metal Hydride (MH) reactors, especially those based on magnesium, have garnered attention for hydrogen storage due to their high charging capacity, density, and operational efficiency [2,3]. In this study, “efficiency” refers specifically to the higher rates of hydrogen charging and discharging within the storage tank. Despite their advantages, these reactors face significant challenges, particularly in the thermal management of hydrogen charging/discharging processes, which release/absorb substantial heat [4,5]. The inherently insufficient thermal conductivity of MH reactors further complicates this, leading to prolonged charging and discharging times and underscoring the importance of optimizing the thermal performance of hydrogen storage systems [6,7].

In recent decades, substantial research efforts have been dedicated to enhancing the heat and mass transfer dynamics of MH tanks, both internally [8,9] and externally [10,11]. The primary goal has been reducing charging and discharging times, thereby improving system efficiency. It is worth noting that the literature uses various terms for hydrogen absorption/desorption in MH beds, including charging/discharging and hydrogenation/dehydrogenation. In this paper, “charging” and “discharging” refer to the absorption and desorption of hydrogen molecules by the metal bed. Various strategies have been explored, including increasing the effective thermal conductivity of MH tanks [12,13], integrating fins made from different materials [14,15], and incorporating heat exchangers within the MH tanks [16,17].

Recently, there has been growing interest in using helical coil heat exchangers in MH tanks. These systems significantly enhance heat transfer due to the secondary circulation they induce [18,19]. Raju and Kumar [20] evaluated various thermal management strategies for MH hydrogen storage systems to meet the demanding refuelling targets of 3–5 min in automotive applications. They compared three heat exchanger designs: a shell-and-tube heat exchanger with alienate in the shell, a helical coil heat exchanger, and a shell-and-tube heat exchanger with alienate in the tubes. Their results indicated that the helical coil design outperformed the other configurations by 15% in terms of gravimetric and volumetric densities, making it a preferred choice for MH beds. Eisapour et al. [21] further supported this finding through numerical analysis, showing that incorporating a helical heat exchanger with a central return tube in a magnesium-based MH reactor significantly enhanced heat transfer, reduced the reactor's average temperature, and accelerated hydrogen charging. Optimization studies identified specific parameters (tube diameter (5 mm), coil diameter (18 mm), and coil pitch (10 mm)) that led to a 24% reduction in charging time, reaching 90% storage capacity, compared to a conventional helical coil without a central return tube. In another study, Larpruenrudee et al. [22] introduced a semi-cylindrical coil heat exchanger with a central return tube (SCHE-CR) to address the issue of inadequate heat transfer in the central regions of the storage system. This innovative configuration reduced absorption duration by 30% compared to conventional designs. Further enhancements, such as increasing tube diameters and incorporating external cooling jackets, led to reductions of 40% and 51%, respectively.

Additional studies confirm the effectiveness of coiled heat exchangers in enhancing MH storage performance. Krishna et al. [23] investigated a novel flat coil tube heat exchanger with a spiral fin to improve heat transfer in LaNi5-based metal hydride systems. Their innovative design achieved a 35.3% and 16.7% reduction in the time required for 90% hydrogen storage compared to conventional single and double helical tube heat exchangers, respectively. Wu et al. [24] reported that magnesium-based hydrogen storage systems equipped with coiled heat exchangers demonstrated superior reaction kinetics and heat transfer rates compared to systems using straight cooling tubes. The reactor with a helical coil completed the reaction in 4000 s, while the reactor with a straight tube heat exchanger did not reach full completion within the same time frame. Similarly, Li et al. [25] and Tong et al. [26] conducted numerical and simulation studies comparing straight and coiled tube configurations. Their findings consistently demonstrated the enhanced performance of coiled heat exchangers, particularly in dual helical reactor designs.

Traditionally, coiled heat exchangers in MH storage tanks have adopted a helical shape. However, recent developments in conical-shaped coils suggest a promising alternative. These novel designs can potentially leverage variations in boundary and initial conditions, leading to higher heat transfer rates [27]. Purandare et al. [28] conducted an experimental analysis of conical coil heat exchangers, fabricating and testing coils with different cone angles (0° (helical), 45°, 90°, 135°, and 180° (spiral)) and tube sizes. Their findings revealed that the helical coil exhibited the highest Nusselt number among the studied configurations, while the spiral coil demonstrated the lowest. Singh and Sarkar [29] investigated a concentric tube heat exchanger with conical wire coil inserts using water-based nanofluids as the working medium. Their experimental results highlighted the superior thermohydraulic performance of diverging wire coils, achieving a maximum Nusselt number enhancement of 171% compared to a plain tube. Recent studies by Missaoui [30] and Mao et al. [31] further validated the effectiveness of conical coils in enhancing thermal management in specific heat exchanger applications. Unlike traditional helixes, conical coils offer increased surface area in regions with higher heat accumulation, providing greater cooling flexibility. This is especially valuable for MH tanks exhibiting temperature disparities across different areas. Despite the promising improvements of certain types of conical-shaped coils over helical coils in enhancing heat transfer rates, as reported in the literature, their impact on hydrogen storage tanks has not yet been analyzed.

The exothermic and endothermic reactions associated with hydrogen charging/discharging release/absorb significant heat, which, if not effectively managed, can impede hydrogen storage efficiency and raise safety concerns. Phase Change Materials (PCMs) offer a promising solution for thermal management in MH tanks by absorbing or releasing large amounts of latent heat during phase transitions, thereby stabilizing the tank's temperature. Integrating PCMs into MH tank designs can absorb excess heat during hydrogen charging and release stored heat during discharging, improving system efficiency. Ardahaie et al. [32] demonstrated that a magnesium-based MH tank integrated with PCMs and a flat spiral tube heat exchanger exhibited up to 44% enhanced charging and 20% improved discharging performance compared to non-PCM systems. Larpruenrudee et al. [33] focused on enhancing the thermal performance of metal hydride storage systems through improved heat exchanger designs and the incorporation of PCM. The SCHE-CR was found to reduce hydrogen absorption duration by over 50% compared to traditional helical coil designs. PCM integration, particularly with configurations like PCM capsules, further boosted performance, decreasing cycle durations by at least 39%. Dong et al. [34] proposed a novel honeycomb fin reactor design, incorporating sandwich PCM to enhance heat transfer during hydrogen charging and discharging. Compared to a reactor where PCM is only filled around the periphery of the honeycomb fins, this configuration significantly improved performance, reducing hydrogen charging and discharging times by approximately 86.4% and 81.1%, respectively. Ye et al. [35] used an array layout in PCM-based MH reactors to increase the heat transfer interface with a high specific surface area. This design resulted in a significant increase in heat transfer area, although the impact on absorption efficiency was limited by thermal resistance in both the MH and PCM. Nonetheless, the array reactor demonstrated a 1.0 times higher absorption rate compared to traditional reactors, driven by a 1.6 times expansion in the heat transfer area. Further improvements in heat transfer and absorption efficiency were observed when the thermal conductivities of MH and PCM were increased, with absorption rates rising by 58.5% and a factor of 1.2, respectively. In another study, Ye et al. [36] developed a mathematical model to analyze the transfer and reaction processes in MH-PCM reactors highlighting several key factors influencing performance. The study found that while increasing PCM volume and latent heat had limited effects on enhancing heat transfer, improving PCM thermal conductivity and hydrogen supply pressure significantly boosted the heat transfer rate and absorption efficiency. Furthermore, the research established a quantitative relationship between volumetric hydrogen storage capacity, PCM volume fraction, and thermophysical properties, indicating that optimizing hydrogen absorption pressure could maximize PCM's sensible heat storage and enhance reactor performance.

This literature review highlights the critical role of coiled heat exchangers in improving the thermal management of MH tanks by accelerating hydrogen charging and discharging. While using conical coils and PCMs has shown promise in other applications, there are limited studies on their application in MH storage, mainly representing a critical research gap. Given the importance of efficient heat exchangers in MH tanks, it is imperative to investigate the performance of conical coils and PCM integration for thermal management, comparing them with traditional helical coil designs. This study aims to improve MH hydrogen storage efficiency by integrating a conical heat exchanger for enhanced thermal management and a PCM jacket to regulate heat during charging and discharging. The research progresses in four stages: (1) a comparative analysis of MH tanks with helical and conical coils; (2) optimization of the top-performing configuration with a PCM jacket, testing various jacket diameters to examine the impact of PCM mass on charging; (3) sensitivity analyses of hydrogen pressure, Heat Transfer Fluid (HTF) inlet temperature, and Reynolds number to evaluate their effects on charging; and (4) assessment of the optimized configuration during discharging. These findings offer valuable insights for advancing hydrogen storage technology.

This section outlines the MH tanks' configuration, detailing the PCM's thermophysical properties and the system characterization employed in this study. Fig. 1 presents the methodological framework for optimizing the conical coil-assisted MH tank design. The process includes integrating a PCM jacket to improve hydrogen storage performance, specifically through evaluating hydrogen charging and discharging rates.

Following validating the numerical solution and selecting a reliable numerical approach, as detailed in Section 2.6, the study examines the impact of various coil configurations. Traditionally, helical coils are used to facilitate heat removal during hydrogen charging and heat delivery during discharging. This research evaluates conical coils with different design characteristics, comparing them to the conventional helical coil. The conical coil configuration provides an expanded contact surface area for the HTF in regions requiring more significant cooling while reducing the surface area in areas with lower cooling demand in charging mode. Since hydrogen is delivered into the MH bed from the bottom, the conical coil features a wider diameter at the base, where the charging rate is higher due to the reaction initiation. This design allows for optimal heat management across the tank.

The impact of incorporating a PCM jacket is explored following the coil design enhancement. PCM is selected for its high thermal capacity, which helps manage excessive temperature rises within the MH bed. During charging, the PCM absorbs excess heat, preventing the MH bed from overheating and leading to phase transition in the PCM. The stored thermal energy is subsequently released during the discharging process, accelerating hydrogen discharging, as charging is exothermic and discharging is endothermic. The amount of PCM used is critical to the system's overall performance. Different PCM jacket thicknesses are tested to identify the optimal configuration. Sensitivity analyses are then performed to evaluate the effect of operating conditions on the charging process, such as the Reynolds number, HTF inlet temperature, and hydrogen pressure. Finally, the system design is assessed under discharging conditions to evaluate the performance of the proposed MH tank during both the charging and discharging phases.

Fig. 2 presents the dimensional parameters used to define the different MH storage tank configurations investigated in this study. Six cases (A to F) are generated by systematically varying these parameters, each representing a unique tank design. The corresponding dimensional values for these configurations are listed in Table 1 .

Table 1 summarizes the dimensional characteristics of the MH tanks with both helical and conical coiled heat exchangers, along with the PCM jacket. It is important to note that the MH bed volume is constant across all cases to ensure a meaningful comparison of hydrogen storage performance. As a result, the tube diameter, d
t , and the tube wall thickness are varied to accommodate the design constraints and the PCM jacket is added around the storage tank to maintain consistency in the amount of absorbed hydrogen and heat generated during the charging process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of materials for hydrogen storage and thermal management
ce:section.ce:para: This study selects a magnesium-based alloy, Mg₂Ni, for the MH hydrogen storage tank due to its superior hydrogen storage capacity and favourable thermodynamic properties. Mg₂Ni can absorb hydrogen to form Mg₂NiH₄, offering a hydrogen storage capacity of approximately 3.6 wt% [37]. This alloy also operates at relatively lower discharging temperatures than pure magnesium hydride, making it more practical for real-world applications. Furthermore, Mg₂Ni demonstrates enhanced hydrogen absorption and desorption kinetics, resulting in more efficient hydrogen charging and discharging rates. These characteristics position Mg₂Ni as a highly promising material for sustainable and efficient hydrogen storage systems in energy applications [38]. The thermo-physical properties of the selected MH and hydrogen are detailed in Table 2 . Air is chosen as the HTF for its abundance, low cost, and ease of implementation. Compared to liquid coolants, air eliminates the risks of fluid leakage and corrosion, thereby simplifying the system design. The thermophysical properties of air were evaluated at the inlet temperature specified in Table 2.

For thermal management, NaNO₃ (sodium nitrate) is chosen as the PCM due to its significant advantages over other PCMs, such as FeCl₃ and NaOH, particularly in magnesium-based MH systems like Mg₂Ni [32]. NaNO₃ offers high thermal conductivity and a melting point of 579 K, which align well with the operational temperatures of magnesium-based hydrides. This ensures efficient heat charging during hydrogen uptake and effective heat release during discharging, optimizing the tank's performance. Additionally, NaNO₃ exhibits stable thermal cycling behavior, which is crucial for the long-term durability and performance stability of MH tanks [44]. Compared to organic PCMs, NaNO₃‘s higher thermal conductivity and heat capacity make it a more suitable candidate for enhancing heat transfer, improving hydrogen release rates, and increasing the system's overall energy efficiency. Moreover, its non-toxic nature, widespread availability, and cost-effectiveness further strengthen its suitability for sustainable, large-scale applications in clean energy systems. The thermo-physical properties of NaNO₃ are provided in Table 3 .

This section outlines the governing equations of the charging and discharging process and heat transfer within the MH and turbulence modeling. The following assumptions are made to simplify the model:
●
A dimensionless parameter, N, is utilized to evaluate the relative error in storage time when the flow equation is excluded in the reactor bed analysis. It is defined as:

When N is less than 0.1, the fluid flow can be neglected in hydrogen tank simulations without introducing significant errors. In this study, N, is well below this threshold, indicating that the influence of pressure gradients is negligible.
●
Radiation heat transfer within the MH is disregarded [49].
●
Hydrogen and MH maintain constant thermophysical properties throughout hydrogen charging and discharging [50].
●
Hydrogen is treated as an ideal gas, thus applying the local thermal equilibrium condition to the system [51,52].
●
The outer shell is well-insulated, effectively preventing any heat transfer from the outer shell to the environment [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Governing equations for the phase change material (PCM)
ce:section.ce:para: This research treats the liquid PCM as an incompressible Newtonian fluid, and viscous dissipation is disregarded. The phase change process is simulated using the enthalpy-porosity method, while the governing equations for mass, momentum, and energy conservation are utilized to describe the system:

A source term, introduced into the momentum equation, represents Darcy's law damping term, which accounts for the phase change effect in convective heat transfer. This term is expressed as follows [72,73]:

The initial conditions in both the MH tank and the PCM before the start of the charging process are assumed to be uniform, with an average hydrogen reaction fraction of 0.043. It is also considered that the outer boundary of the MH tank is completely insulated. The initial and boundary conditions are as follows:

ce:para.ce:bold: On the inner surface of the pipe:

ce:section.ce:para: The temperature distribution in the domain at the beginning of the discharging process is not reinitialized; instead, the values at the end of the charging process are used as the initial conditions for the discharging process, as defined by Equation (21). The turbulence intensity at the HTF tube inlet is set at 5%, based on the assumption that the flow entering the computational domain originates from a straight pipe with fully developed turbulent airflow [78]. For a detailed illustration of the boundary conditions applied in this study, refer to Fig. 3 .

A grid independence study is conducted to ensure reliable results by testing various grid sizes and time steps before the simulation. Fig. 4 shows the computational mesh used for the MH hydrogen storage tank, specifically for Case E. The same meshing techniques and structured grid types are applied across all cases, with minimal variation in total grid numbers between cases. As such, Case E is selected to perform a detailed grid independence analysis for each type of geometrical model.

Table 4 highlights the impact of different time steps and grid densities on the hydrogen charging time for Case E. Four time steps (0.25 s, 0.15 s, 0.05 s, and 0.01 s) are tested to verify time step independence, all using the same mesh configuration. The optimal time step is found to be 0.05 s, as shown in the shaded area of Table 4, with further reductions in the time step having negligible impact on the charging time.

Additionally, as shown in Table 4, the charging time stabilized beyond 4,570,000 grids, indicating no significant variation with further increases in grid number. Therefore, a grid size of 4,570,000 is selected for all cases to balance accuracy and computational efficiency. It is noteworthy that the mesh density and quality in the region adjacent to the tube wall are critical for accurate simulations. To resolve the flow characteristics near the inner wall of the tube effectively, a fine inflationary mesh comprising 10 layers with a growth rate of 1.2 was employed in this region.
The same grid characteristics are applied to other cases based on the presented independence checks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: User defined function (UDF) code validation for metal hydride
ce:section.ce:para: The magnesium-based MH reactor analyzed in this study is validated for both hydrogen charging and discharging processes. The simulation code used is benchmarked against two experimental studies that employed magnesium-based metal hydrides for hydrogen storage and release. For validation purposes, the results from Muthukumar et al. [79] are utilized for the charging phase, while those from Wu et al. [65] are used for the discharging phase.

Muthukumar et al. [79] conducted experimental work on hydrogen storage using mechanically alloyed Mg2Ni. The alloy is synthesized through mechanical milling and subsequently annealed at 753 K under an inert argon atmosphere. The experiments are performed at a temperature of 573 K and a supply pressure of 2 MPa. For discharging process validation in the study of Wu et al. [65], the storage system included Mg2Ni powder and a helical coil heat exchanger, with an initial temperature of 573 K and a hydrogen pressure of 1.6 atm. As illustrated in Fig. 5 (a) and (b), the temperature predictions produced by the current simulation closely align with the available data in the literature, confirming excellent agreement.

For this case study, the SST k−ω turbulence model has been chosen. To validate this selection, the current numerical model's results are compared with experimental data from Colombo et al. [80] which comprehensively analyses fully developed single-phase turbulent flow and pressure drops in helically coiled channels. The experimental friction factors are utilized to assess the performance of different turbulence models using the CFD software FLUENT, aiming to evaluate their accuracy in predicting the friction factor and wall shear stress in helically coiled channels.

The transition to turbulence in helically coiled pipes occurs gradually, with the Reynolds number required to achieve fully turbulent conditions being higher compared to straight pipes [81]. According to Colombo et al. [80], two critical Reynolds numbers are proposed to characterize the onset and completion of turbulence emergence:
(25)
R e

(
D c

(26)

In this study, the Reynolds number is consistently 9000, which exceeds Re
1 for all cases, indicating the flow is in the transitional regime. As suggested by Colombo et al. [80], the SST k−
ω
turbulence model provides higher accuracy within this Reynolds number range. Validation of the simulation model against the experimental results of Colombo et al. [80], as shown in Fig. 6 , confirms that the SST k−
ω
model offers the highest reliability across the studied Reynolds numbers. Consequently, this model has been selected for the present study.

The experimental studies conducted by Al-Abidi et al. [82] and Hosseini et al. [83] are utilized to validate the numerical model applied to the PCM subdomain in the current case study. Additionally, the findings from Alqahtani et al. [84] are also validated against the present numerical model. Alqahtani et al. [84] examined the thermal behavior of an MH reactor surrounded by NaNO3, the same PCM material employed in this study.

As illustrated in Fig. 7 (a) and (b), the current numerical model accurately aligns with the experimental data for the average temperature of the PCM inside the shell and tube heat exchanger during both the melting and solidification phases reported by Hosseini et al. [83]. Additionally, the model effectively reproduces the results of Alqahtani et al. [84], as depicted in Fig. 7 (c).

The simulations in this study are conducted using the commercial software ANSYS Fluent 2023 R2. Custom user-defined functions (UDFs) are developed in C programming language and integrated into the software to compute the kinetic behavior of hydrogen charging and discharging during the respective processes. The power-law differencing scheme is employed to solve the momentum and energy equations [85]. Additionally, the PISO method [86] and the PRESTO scheme [85] are implemented for pressure-velocity coupling and pressure correction, respectively.

The under-relaxation factors are set to 0.4 for pressure, 0.4 for momentum, 0.8 for liquid fraction, and 0.8 for energy to ensure stable iteration. The convergence of the solution is monitored at the end of each time step, with a convergence criterion of 10−6 applied to all variables.

While the numerical model exhibits strong agreement with experimental results, it is essential to recognize its limitations and potential areas for improvement. This discussion provides a transparent evaluation of the model's strengths and weaknesses. One potential source of error arises from discrepancies between the numerical model and experimental data across different simulation configurations. Since the physical system has not yet been constructed and no experimental data is available for uncertainty analysis, the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of the current model for key processes; charging/discharging of the MH tank, PCM melting/solidification, and turbulence modeling in the coil are 2.4%, 2.0%, and 5.0%, respectively, based on the presented numerical and experimental comparisons in section 2.6. Another limitation of the model is the omission of long-term PCM thermal behavior. The authors acknowledge this constraint and recommend future studies to experimentally validate PCM thermal stability over multiple hydrogen charging/discharging cycles.

The results are organized into four key subsections: (1) a comparative analysis between helical coil and conical coil heat exchangers, each with varying design characteristics; (2) the incorporation of a PCM jacket with different thicknesses to enhance thermal management by storing the heat generated during the charging process for reuse in discharging; (3) once the optimal coil design and PCM configuration are identified, a sensitivity analysis is conducted on the MH hydrogen storage system to evaluate the influence of operating parameters on the charging rate; and (4) the best-performing configuration is simulated during the discharging phase to assess both charging and discharging times, providing a comprehensive evaluation of the system's performance in hydrogen uptake and release.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of conical coil heat exchangers
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 illustrates the Average Bed Temperature (ABT) and Hydrogen Concentration (HC) of the MH porous bed over time for Cases A, B, and C. The results indicate that the hydrogen charging time for Case A, which employs a helical coil heat exchanger, is 12,100 s. In contrast, Cases B and C achieve hydrogen charging times of 8490 s and 8550 s, respectively. This represents approximately a 30% reduction in charging time compared to the helical coil design of Case A. The primary reason for this improvement lies in the more effective reduction of the ABT in Cases B and C, enabling more efficient heat removal from the MH bed and, consequently, faster hydrogen charging.

To provide a deeper understanding of the hydrogen charging process and the interplay between HC and ABT, contour plots of these parameters are presented in Figs. 9 and 10
. Fig. 9 displays the HC for Cases A, B, and C at three distinct time intervals: 1800 s, 5400 s, and 9000 s. In the early stages of the charging process, HC is higher in the central region of the MH bed in Case A, where the HTF tube follows a helical pattern rather than a straight one. This is expected, as the increased surface area of the helical coil promotes greater heat transfer compared to a straight tube. HC rises throughout the MH bed as charging progresses, but significant differences in charging behavior emerge among the three cases.

Fig. 11 , illustrating the ABT for each case, sheds light on the reasons behind these variations. In Case A, hydrogen is absorbed more rapidly at the top of the MH bed, as this is where the HTF inlet is located, and the temperature gradient between the HTF and MH bed is greatest. However, a conical coil design is employed in Cases B and C to enhance heat removal. The coil's maximum diameter is positioned at the bottom of the MH tank, where the HTF has traversed most of the coil, and its temperature has increased. This strategic placement of the larger coil diameter maximizes the heat transfer surface area in this region, as evidenced by the improved heat removal in Cases B and C compared to Case A. Consequently, these cases achieve shorter charging times, aligning with this study's hydrogen storage design objectives.

Despite these improvements, there is a notable difference between the ABT contours for Cases B and C, which in turn influences the HC distributions. In Case C, although the coil diameter is larger at the bottom of the tank, providing a greater heat transfer surface area than in Case B, it limits cooling the central region at the bottom of the MH bed. This suggests that simply increasing the coil diameter does not always translate into improved performance. While a larger diameter enhances heat transfer to a point, it can become counterproductive if the ability to cool critical areas, such as the center of the MH bed, is compromised. As a result, although Case C has a higher heat transfer area (as shown in Table 5 ), its performance in terms of charging rate is similar to that of Case B.

Table 5 provides a detailed comparison of the relative pressure drops for each coil design. Notably, Case C exhibits a significantly higher pressure drop compared to Case B, even though both demonstrate similar performance during the charging process. Consequently, Case B is identified as the more advantageous design, as it offers an optimal balance between heat transfer efficiency and pressure drop.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of adding PCM jacket and thickness
ce:section.ce:para: This section compares the hydrogen charging times for Case B with and without a PCM jacket. As shown in Table 1, Cases D, E, and F feature PCM jackets with diameters of 70 mm, 90 mm, and 110 mm, respectively, to evaluate the effect of PCM thickness on MH hydrogen storage tank performance. Fig. 11(a) illustrates the HC and ABT of these cases over time. Cases with PCM jackets exhibit significantly shorter charging times compared to Case B, which lacks one. Specifically, Cases D, E, and F achieve a hydrogen concentration of 0.9 in 3,390, 2,710, and 2640 s, respectively, while Case A requires 8490 s. This corresponds to a 60%, 68%, and 69% reduction in charging time for Cases D, E, and F, respectively. The substantial improvement is attributed to the PCM's enhanced heat removal from the MH bed.

Fig. 11(b) further illustrates the liquid fraction and average temperature of the PCM jackets in Cases D, E, and F. In Case D, with a PCM jacket diameter of 70 mm, the liquid fraction exceeds 0.90 by the end of the charging process, indicating that most of the PCM's latent heat capacity is utilized. In contrast, for Cases E and F, which have larger PCM jacket diameters, the liquid fraction reaches 0.56 and 0.34, respectively. This lower liquid fraction correlates with lower average PCM temperatures in Cases E and F compared to Case D. These variations explain the differences in hydrogen charging times among the cases with different PCM thicknesses.

To further investigate the effects of PCM temperature and liquid fraction on charging time, Fig. 12 presents the temperature, hydrogen concentration, and liquid fraction contours for Cases D, E, and F after 2400 s of charging. The use of PCM is advantageous because when a suitable material such as NaNO₃ is selected with the appropriate phase transition temperature range, the PCM can maintain a nearly constant temperature while absorbing heat during the phase change process [32]. Due to the high latent heat of PCMs, they absorb substantial amounts of heat without a significant increase in temperature. As a result, if the PCM material and thickness are appropriately chosen, the charging process can be significantly enhanced.

As shown in Fig. 11, after 2400 s, most of the PCM in Case D has melted, with only a small portion remaining solid at the bottom. This indicates that the liquid phase of the PCM in Case D absorbs heat, which leads to an increase in average PCM temperature, as shown in the temperature contours. The charging rate decreases if the MH system cannot remove heat effectively. This explains why Case D, which has the smallest PCM jacket, exhibits lower hydrogen charging performance than Cases E and F. In Cases E and F, the increased PCM volume provides greater latent heat capacity, allowing more heat to be absorbed, as evidenced by the lower bed temperatures observed in these cases compared to Case D.

However, when comparing Cases E and F, it becomes clear that increasing the PCM jacket diameter beyond 90 mm (as in Case F) does not result in further improvements in charging performance. The top region of the PCM melts more quickly than the bottom due to natural convection and buoyancy forces, which dominate heat transfer within the PCM jacket.

Sensitivity analyses are performed on the HTF inlet temperature, HTF Reynolds number, and hydrogen inlet pressure to evaluate the effects of varying operating conditions on the hydrogen charging process in the proposed MH hydrogen storage system. It is important to emphasize that the optimal operating conditions should be selected based on the specific limitations and objectives of each hydrogen storage application. This sensitivity analysis aims to highlight how the results vary with changes in the operational parameters. These analyses are based on Case E, which demonstrates the best charging performance in previous sections. Table 6 outlines the operating conditions tested for Case E, with the base operating conditions highlighted. Each parameter's lower and upper values are considered relative to the base conditions.

Fig. 13 presents the ABT and HC over time for three different HTF inlet temperatures: 523 K, 573 K (the base case), and 623 K. The results show that increasing the HTF inlet temperature by 50 K from the base value (573 K) increases the charging time from 2710 s to 4450 s, representing a 64% increase. Conversely, reducing the HTF inlet temperature by 50 K decreases the charging time from 2710 to 2070 s, resulting in a 23% reduction. This suggests that an increase in HTF inlet temperature has a more pronounced effect on charging time than a decrease. However, it is essential to note that while lower HTF inlet temperatures may favour faster charging, it is impractical to use significantly lower HTF temperatures than the MH bed temperature, which remains above 573 K during charging. Doing so could risk thermal shock and compromise material integrity. The base HTF inlet temperature is adopted from the experimental studies of Liang et al. [81] and Chung et al. [82].

Fig. 14 illustrates the effect of varying the Reynolds number by ±2000 from the base value of 9000 in Case E. Reducing the Reynolds number to 7000 increases the charging time from 2710 s to 2900 s, a 7% increase. Conversely, increasing the Reynolds number to 11,000 reduces the charging time from 2710 s to 2560 s, a 6% decrease. Although increasing the Reynolds number improves charging time, the energy demands of higher HTF flow rates should be considered. Given the modest gains observed, future studies should assess whether this improvement is energy-efficient and economically viable.

Fig. 15 shows the impact of hydrogen inlet pressure on charging time for Case E. The hydrogen inlet pressure is varied by ±0.4 MPa from the base value of 1.6 MPa. Lowering the hydrogen pressure to 1.2 MPa increases the charging time from 2710 s to 3570 s, a 31% increase. On the other hand, raising the pressure to 2.0 MPa decreases the charging time from 2710 s to 2210 s, an 18% reduction. These results indicate that while increasing hydrogen pressure decreases charging time, the rate of improvement diminishes at higher pressures. Therefore, designers must carefully weigh the energy costs of increasing pressure against the desired improvement in charging time when optimizing the MH hydrogen storage tank.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charging and discharging process in the proposed design (case E)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 16 presents the ABT and HC over time for the best-performing configuration in this study, Case E, which combines a conical coil heat exchanger design with a PCM jacket. The simulations are conducted under the base operating conditions: HTF inlet fluid temperature of 573 K, Reynolds number of 9,000, and hydrogen inlet pressure of 1.6 MPa. At the start of the charging process, the ABT rapidly increases from 573 K to a peak of 644 K, followed by a gradual decrease as the HTF and PCM remove heat from the MH bed. The ABT then stabilizes around 610 K at the end of the charging process when an HC of 0.9 is reached. During the discharging process, the ABT sharply drops from 610 K to approximately 515 K, after which heat is transferred back from the HTF and PCM to the MH bed to sustain the discharging kinetics. The PCM effectively stores excess heat generated during charging and releases it during discharging, functioning as a thermal battery.

Fig. 17 depicts the PCM jacket's average temperature and liquid fraction during the charging and discharging processes in Case E. During charging, the average PCM temperature rises to 587 K, with a liquid fraction of approximately 0.55. Although the PCM temperature exceeds its melting point (580 K), the liquid fraction remains below 1, indicating incomplete utilization of the PCM's latent heat capacity. This highlights the potential for improved designs to enhance latent heat usage, which future studies could address. During discharging, the stored heat is released back to the MH bed, with the PCM functioning as a thermal regulator. As discharging begins, the PCM temperature and liquid fraction decrease to around 569 K and 0.22, respectively. Despite the temperature falling below the solidification point (579 K), the PCM remains partially molten, suggesting further opportunities to optimize the design for maximizing latent heat recovery and preventing overheating or overcooling.

This study explored the integration of a conical heat exchanger and a phase change material (PCM) jacket within metal hydride (MH) hydrogen storage systems to address thermal management challenges and improve the overall charging/discharging rate. Initially, the performance of the conical coil heat exchanger is compared to conventional helical coil designs. Subsequently, a PCM jacket with three different diameters is incorporated to store the heat released during charging and reuse it in the discharging phase. Finally, sensitivity analyses are conducted to evaluate the impact of the heat transfer fluid's Reynolds number, inlet temperature, and hydrogen inlet pressure. The key conclusions drawn from the study are as follows:
●
A novel conical heat exchanger design reduced hydrogen charging time by approximately 30% compared to traditional helical coils. This improvement is primarily attributed to the conical shape, which optimizes heat transfer by varying the surface area to meet cooling demands.
●
Integrating a PCM jacket further reduced hydrogen charging time, achieving up to a 69% improvement compared to systems without PCM. The PCM's ability to store excess heat during the exothermic charging process and release it during the endothermic discharging phase enhances thermal management. This highlights the pivotal role of PCM jackets in improving hydrogen storage charging/discharging rate.
●
Sensitivity analyses indicated that hydrogen inlet pressure, heat transfer fluid temperature, and Reynolds number are critical factors influencing system performance. A rise in hydrogen inlet pressure reduced charging time by 18% while optimizing heat transfer fluid conditions further improved the system charging/discharging rate.
●
The proposed design achieved hydrogen charging and discharging in approximately 7000 s, compared to 12,100 s for conventional systems under the same operating conditions, representing a significant improvement.
Future research could focus on multi-objective optimization, considering charging/discharging times, energy consumption, pressure drop, long-term thermal behavior of the PCM and overall system cost.
